mapping.a_element: quasispecies model of molecular evolution with genetic recombination/horizontal gene transfer
mapping.b_element: co-evolutionary quasispecies model of virus and immune response

mapping.a_element: steady-state mean fitness and distribution of individuals in a population
mapping.b_element: viral error catastrophes and adaptation regimes in co-evolving quasispecies

mapping.a_element: maximum principle for equilibrium mean fitness
mapping.b_element: constraints on B-cell mutation rates to minimize allowed viral mutation rates

mapping.a_element: fitness landscape with positive/negative epistasis affecting selection
mapping.b_element: viral mutation rate regimes leading to error or adaptation catastrophes

mapping.a_element: analytic field theoretic mapping for solvability

brief_justification: While both papers use quasispecies theory, Paper A focuses on the solvability and phase transitions of recombination/horizontal gene transfer in a single population via a maximum principle and field-theoretic mapping, whereas Paper B studies co-evolutionary dynamics between two coupled quasispecies (virus and immune response) to identify mutation rate regimes causing error catastrophes. The core mechanisms—recombination-driven fitness optimization vs. co-evolutionary catastrophe minimization—are distinct.
